data.title: Lundin Mining: The Copper Bull Case Is Strong -- But Here's The Catch
data.description:
Overview

Lundin Mining Corporation (LUNMF)(LNDMY) is a diversified base metals mining company headquartered in Canada. The business model of Lundin is based on organic growth through expansion and optimization of existing mines, strategic acquisitions meant to expand the production life and asset base. Lundin also has joint ventures with players like BHP which allow for synergistic operations, and all this is managed keeping in mind disciplined capital allocation.

In one of my previous articles, I had written on Taseko Mines about how it was likely to benefit from the copper boom. Here we look at how Lundin may benefit from the different supply-demand forces.

Lundin's portfolio includes operating assets in Candelaria, and Caserones, both in Chile, and Chapada in Brazil. It also has the Vicuña project in Argentina, which is in a 50% joint venture with BHP. The Vicuña is said to be among one of the largest copper discoveries in the past 30 years and is expected to rank among the world’s top copper, gold, and silver mines. The Vicuña project is the single most important driver of Lundin’s long-term valuation and growth trajectory.

In recent times, Lundin has sharpened its focus to become a pure-play copper-mining player. This has been visible after Lundin deliberately sold the mines of Neves-Corvo and Zinkgruvan to Boliden and divested Eagle Mine to Talon Metals. This is meant to enhance operational efficiency and align the company with long-term copper demand. According to the earnings report and company MD&A, Lundin aims to be among the top 10 global copper producers.

One of the primary reasons for Lundin being bullish on copper is that it is a key metal for the ongoing energy transition, and it’s a metal used in electrification, digital infrastructure, etc. The multiple demand sources ensure that demand for copper is likely to remain robust, and any pure-play copper miner and producer is likely to benefit from the upside as well. The miner in January had cut guidance for gold and copper production, which highlights the growing importance of copper for the business of Lundin.

Lundin’s copper production is anchored by three core operations. The Candelaria mine in Chile operates a processing plant with a capacity of approximately 75,000–80,000 tonnes per day and produces over 140,000 tonnes of copper annually. The Caserones mine, also in Chile, has an even larger processing capacity of around 105,000 tonnes per day and contributes over 130,000 tonnes of copper annually, making it a key growth driver following Lundin’s increased ownership stake. In Brazil, the Chapada mine operates at approximately 65,000 tonnes per day and produces around 40,000–45,000 tonnes of copper annually.

The impact of the strategic moves has also been largely reflected in the share price trend, as Lundin has generated a solid 89.48% return in the past six months alone, as shown in the price chart below.

Tailwind

Strategic Industries Driving Copper Demand

The global trend towards energy transition is going to prove to be a major source of copper demand. According to a report by EY, in order to meet the global electrification goals in the upcoming 30 years, 115% more copper needs to be mined.

The copper market is tight, with deficits expected to worsen after 2025 due to scarce new production

The graph below highlights the complete picture of the developing supply-demand gap that is expected to develop due to the slower pace of copper discoveries and longer time-to-production. The current hotspot of copper mine exploration is South America, which accounts for 44% of the global capex for exploration. Even in South America, the global hotspot for exploration is Chile, which accounts for 20%. It is clear that Lundin is at the very center of the hotspot. Moreover, a study by Wood Mackenzie found that there is likely to be a 10 million tonne copper deficit by 2028.

Chile is the largest copper-producing country in the world and remains the epicenter of large-scale copper mining due to its resource quality and established infrastructure. Lundin’s positioning in Chile through its Candelaria and Caserones assets places it directly within this high-grade copper belt.

The Candelaria complex is a long-life, large-scale operation and serves as a stable production and cash flow base for Lundin. Meanwhile, the Caserones mine has become increasingly important to the company's strategy, with Lundin increasing its stake by 5%, which reinforces its commitment to copper-production. Apart from this, the Vicuña project presents a unique opportunity for Lundin, as explained in the subsequent sections.

This has been gradually reflected in the prices as well. The chart below shows the 5 year copper futures price movement, which has seen a gain of 45.36% and is expected to move up further.

The reason for copper demand outpacing supply is the rising demand for copper from some of the most strategic industries.

The EV market is one of the fastest-growing markets, and the growth of the EV market will essentially feed into the demand for copper. The reason is highlighted below, as I had previously highlighted in my article on Taseko Mines.

The high copper content per vehicle is likely to drive up the demand for copper as EVs become more popular. Therefore, it is expected that a copper player like Lundin is likely to benefit from the upcoming supply crunch.

Another avenue of demand for copper is energy transition and electricity, as copper is known for its electricity conduction. With global geopolitical uncertainty in the background, countries are gradually making a transition towards alternate sources of energy like wind and solar. However, the amount of copper used differs for each one. The graph below highlights the same.

However, what is worth noting first is that copper is used in all energy sources, and therefore, a rise in electrification will inevitably lead to a rise in demand for copper. A report by S&P Global states that the world’s electricity consumption will rise by 50%, and based on the graph above, this will raise copper demand as well.

Moreover, the world is also moving towards renewable energy usage as well. According to IEA, renewable sources will account for 46% of energy generation. As the graph shows, the copper consumption of renewable energy sources is higher than non-renewable energy sources. Therefore, we can expect the move towards renewable sources, coupled with global electrification, will lead to a rapid rise in copper demand.

As the shortage becomes more acute and copper prices are expected to rise, manufacturers have explored aluminium as an alternative as well. The table below provides a comparison of the two.

The comparison demonstrates that copper still remains a better choice for manufacturers as it outperforms aluminium on multiple parameters. Therefore, as of today, copper producers and miners can be relatively reassured of the demand for copper across numerous industries.

In addition to the traditional demand drivers, another major source of copper demand is from AI-related infrastructure. The rise of data-centers, cloud computing, and high-performance computing systems is driving significant investment in power-intensive infrastructure.

Data centers require copper wiring for power distribution, cooling systems, and connectivity. This creates a second-order effect where AI growth not only drives direct copper demand from infrastructure but also indirectly boosts demand through energy systems required to sustain it.

The discussion till now has revolved largely around how copper is becoming a strategic metal that is going to experience high demand with insufficient supply. Therefore, Lundin is likely to experience the benefits of the same, given the ongoing trends.

Portfolio Optimization with Policy Support and Strategic Partnership

Amid the tailwind supporting the global copper industry, Lundin has proactively taken steps to capitalize on the same by optimizing its production portfolio to focus on copper mining and production. Previously Lundin used to be a diversified metal mining company. However, Lundin deliberately undertook a move to focus on copper mining. A report by Massif Capital highlights that this move was made from the period of 2020 to 2024. Its share price underperformed compared to its peers, as shown in the diagram below.

While the previous assets were divested, Lundin got into a strategic partnership with BHP for a mine in the Vicuña district. Given the scale and experience of BHP, this provides Lundin with technical expertise and operational excellence practices, enhanced credibility, and lower financial risk.

The Vicuña project has also applied for fiscal stability under the Régimen de Incentivo para Grandes Inversiones (RIGI) of the Argentinian government. This framework is meant to attract large-scale investment by offering long-term tax stability and regulatory certainty. This framework also effectively mitigates sovereign risk and provides long-term economic stability for the Lundin-BHP partnership.

So, the strategic realignment of Lundin amid the global copper tailwind represents a strategic gamble that potentially has a handsome payoff, given that for the upcoming few decades copper will remain a strategic metal for numerous industries, and the upcoming shortage is likely to put upward pressure on the copper prices as well.

Financials & Valuation

To understand the financial impact of the strategic moves that Lundin has made, I decided to perform a financial analysis of the sales and profit margins. The graph below summarises the movement over the past 10 years.

As previously discussed, Lundin made a strategic move towards copper mining, and the rise in revenue post FY20 has been a sign of the same. Moreover, operating profit margins have largely been stable post FY17. However, what remains alarming is the sensitivity of the profit margins to commodity prices. The sharp rise from FY16 to FY17 was because of the end of the commodity cycle downturn, where the peak profit margins in FY21 were due to the commodity price supercycle. While the exposure to commodity prices cannot be eliminated, it can be minimized at best. Therefore, this remains a cause of concern, especially with Lundin choosing to focus on copper.

In order to understand what would be a buying range for investors, I performed a valuation of Lundin. Since it is a mining company and the Vicuña project is going to prove important for the future of Lundin, I performed a sum of the parts valuation to understand the intrinsic value. I performed a relative valuation using the EV/EBITDA ratio to understand the value of the existing business because this is the ratio typically used for mining businesses. To value the Vicuña mine, I used the NPV from the preliminary economic assessment and discounted it by 80% to account for the risk and a further 50% to account for the 50-50 structure of the joint venture of the mine. The summary of the valuation exercise is given below.

The mere 2.64% upside may be disappointing for investors given the strong tailwinds in its favour. However, this is possibly the result of the market having already taken the fundamentals into account. Nonetheless, I would recommend staying invested in Lundin if the investor is looking for long-term gains. Moreover, the valuation exercise has been done without accounting for the external tailwinds in the copper industry, and therefore, I believe that the stock has potential for growth. Therefore, investors can consider cashing in if the price were to fall to $25 or lower.

Headwinds

Dependence on Success of Vicuña Project & Commodity Price Volatility

One of the biggest risks currently facing Lundin is the heavy dependence of its valuation on the success of the Vicuña project because it represents a transformational opportunity for both Lundin and BHP. The project is currently at the Preliminary Economic Assessment (PEA) stage. However, it involves heavy financial, technical, and operational investment. Stage 1 capital expenditure is $7.1 billion, and stages 2 & 3 involve $11.1 billion. A characteristic of this kind of mining project is that it takes years to reach full production levels. This in itself is a source of risk because this provides a window of opportunity to develop alternatives to copper in the different industries, which is likely to dampen the demand for the community. Therefore, the sheer scale of the project and investment may involve high risk, and failure of the project may imply high sunk costs.

In addition to this, another major source of risk is commodity price volatility. In a presentation on the project, a diagram illustrates the sensitivity analysis of the NPV of the project.

As we can see, because of commodity price volatility, the payback period ranges from 5.4 years to 11 years, assuming a discount rate of 8%.

Given the dependence on commodity prices, what is worth noting is that commodity prices depend on a range of variables like global economic growth, infrastructure investments & real estate cycles, prevailing monetary policy, currency fluctuations, etc. With the dependence of commodity prices on numerous such variables, the impact risk becomes more prominent. This is likely to lead to more earnings volatility, which is likely to dampen investor sentiment for Lundin.

Moreover, note that in the short-term, a major headwind for Lundin is that this year there will be a compressed free cash flow in the short term, as the miner is in a "heavy-spend" phase, with $1 billion in total Capex planned for 2026.

Mining Cost Inflation & Deteriorating Ore Grades

Energy costs are expected to account for 30% of the costs of copper mining. Other cost channels include labour, transportation, etc. This is a major source of concern, as energy cost inflation could be a major source of problems for mining companies amid the global geopolitical uncertainty and the rising energy prices.

The graph below shows that costs have largely stabilized in recent times after prolonged cost inflation. Moreover, 90% of copper supply remains at a stable cost of production. So while there are concerns of cost inflation, the relative stability in copper mining is a positive indication. Moreover, given the price level, production also remains profitable.

Another factor in cost inflation is the cost of sulphur going up due to the rising tensions in the Middle East. And the copper mining process in Chile especially is highly dependent on sulphuric acid.

Moreover, a study by S&P Global shows that margins are expected to improve significantly for gold, silver, and copper vis-a-vis other metals, as the graph below illustrates.

This serves as good news given the development of the Vicuña project if Lundin and BHP are able to minimize the time to production and take advantage of the margin expansion. Moreover, with the strategic realignment of the mining portfolio towards copper and away from other base metals, this comes at a crucial time as the margins of the other metals like Nickel, Lithium, etc either contract or margin expansion takes place at a slower rate.

However, while there are positive indications, the concern of cost inflation remains, especially energy costs accounting for 30% of the all-in sustaining cost. Moreover, note that another problem facing copper miners is the problem of degrading copper ore quality. This is a problem that has been acknowledged across studies and is considered a problem almost irreversible. Therefore, the need for refining and purification becomes more important, which is also likely to add to the problem of rising mining costs.

Upcoming Earnings Release

Lundin is expected to release its earnings on May 6, and there will be certain factors to watch out for.

One of the key factors to look out for would be whether Lundin is able to meet its guidance for production or is forced to revise the guidance further. However, it does not stop there. One of the key risks mentioned for Lundin is the problem of cost inflation. Therefore, in the upcoming earnings release, the cash cost will be a key indicator to watch out for. Similarly, any upward revision in the capital expenditure will be another metric to watch out for, as any upward revision is likely to indicate a further cash flow crunch that may be a turn-off for investors.

The two most important factors to watch out for will be the progress on the Vicuña project and the guidance on the copper prices. As previously explained, the Vicuña project is a high-risk, high-reward gamble for Lundin, and the expectations of the management surrounding the project would prove crucial to forming expectations on the share price movement.

Similarly, management guidance on copper prices would also prove important to form expectations on profitability, as the financial analysis showed; profit margins are dependent on copper price cycles.

Conclusion

Lundin Mining Corporation sits at a comfortable juncture today as it is positioned to benefit from energy transition, growing popularity of EVs, rising global infrastructure, and electrification because all these factors lead to increased demand for copper. Lundin has smartly repositioned to focus on copper mining and production. Moreover, Lundin has been smart enough to get into a partnership with an experienced player—BHP. This has come at a time when there is government support in a region that is considered the hotspot of copper mines—South America.

However, Lundin is not without its risks. It currently is in a position where it is exposed to commodity price risk, and the capital-intensive nature of the Vicuña project adds to the same as well. The project is essentially a high-risk, high-reward project. Having said that, copper prices remain high to support all-in sustaining costs, and the risk of the Vicuña project is distributed through a 50-50 joint venture. So, my recommendation for Lundin Mining would be a BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: IPO Calendar Heats Up As AI Chipmaker Cerebras Rejoins The Public Pipeline
data.description:
The IPO market heated up this past week, with five listings raising a combined $4.5 billion. The group featured two billion-dollar deals, including the year’s largest offering to date, as well as the largest US biotech IPO ever. In the pipeline, Cerebras Systems (CBRS) led the week’s additions, filing for an estimated $2 billion offering.

HVAC giant Madison Air Solutions (MAIR) completed the year’s largest IPO to date, pricing at the high end to raise $2.2 billion at a $13.4 billion market cap. The company develops and manufactures mission-critical indoor air quality and air-management technologies for commercial and residential environments, with growing demand from data centers. Madison Air’s backlog more than doubled to $2 billion in 2025, although much of its recent growth has come from acquisitions that have left it highly levered. Madison Air finished the week up 20%.

Defense electronics manufacturer Arxis (ARXS) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $1.1 billion at an $11.6 billion market cap. The company designs and manufactures specialized electronic and mechanical components with applications in aerospace, defense, and space systems, as well as medical technology, semiconductor testing, and industrial automation. Arxis is profitable with expanding EBITDA margins and free cash flow, although it has some revenue concentration and is exposed to the cyclical nature of aerospace and defense spending. Arxis finished up 36%.

Obesity biotech Kailera Therapeutics (KLRA) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $625 million at a $2.0 billion market cap, in the largest US biotech IPO by proceeds ever. Kailera’s lead candidate, ribupatide, is currently being evaluated in global Phase 3 trials as a once-weekly injectable GLP-1/glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor dual agonist peptide. The company is expanding its ribupatide franchise by developing a once-daily oral tablet formulation. Kailera finished up 63%.

Defense systems developer Aevex (AVEX) priced above the midpoint to raise $320 million at a $2.2 billion market cap. Primarily serving the US government, Aevex develops unmanned and autonomous systems for defense applications, with a focus on AI-enabled aerial platforms used in missions such as surveillance, reconnaissance, and precision strike. While it has historically generated a large portion of sales from two programs, the company should continue to benefit from increasing defense spending. Aevex finished up 35%.

Protein biomarker detection platform Alamar Biosciences (ALMR) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $191 million at a $1.2 billion market cap. The company’s proteomics platform combines proprietary instruments, consumables, and software to detect and analyze protein biomarkers at very low concentrations in biological samples such as blood. With a strong base of 300+ customers, Alamar saw revenue and gross margin explode in 2025, though it remains unprofitable. Alamar finished up 29%.

Week Ahead

Four sizable IPOs are currently scheduled in the week ahead, and some smaller issuers may join the calendar throughout the week.

Small modular reactor developer X-Energy (XE) is set to raise $750 million at a $7.1 billion market cap. X-Energy designs advanced nuclear reactor technology, particularly small modular reactors (SMRs), and manufactures nuclear fuel. Its main product, the Xe-100, is a high-temperature gas-cooled reactor designed to generate electricity and heat at a modular scale. The company focuses on developing scalable nuclear systems to help meet growing electricity demand, including from energy-intensive uses like data centers. X-Energy has agreements with a number of blue-chip companies, including Dow, Amazon, and Centrica. However, it has yet to begin construction of its first commercial SMRs, which are not expected to be completed this decade.

Healthcare REIT National Healthcare Properties (NHP) is set to raise $558 million at a $983 million market cap. National Healthcare Properties is a self-managed real estate investment trust that owns a portfolio of senior housing and healthcare properties across 29 states. The company operates through two primary segments: Senior Housing Operating Properties (SHOP; $43mm LTM cash NOI; 85.5% leased; $1.0B gross asset value), which consists of assisted living and memory care communities operated under RIDEA structures, and Outpatient Medical Facilities (OMF; $80mm LTM cash NOI; 92.8% leased; $1.2B gross asset value), which comprises medical office buildings largely affiliated with or adjacent to hospital systems.

Convenience store operator Yesway (YSWY) is set to raise $300 million at a $1.3 billion market cap. Yesway states that it is one of the fastest-growing convenience store operators in the US, with 419 company-operated stores as of December 31, 2025, with 90 successful openings of new stores from 2020 to 2025. The company operates its portfolio primarily under two brands, Yesway and Allsup's. Its sites are differentiated through a leading foodservice offering and a wide variety of grocery items and private-label products. Its geographic footprint consists of stores located in rural and suburban markets across the Midwest and Southwest. Yesway boasts leading fuel margins, with an increasing mix of diesel sold, but sustained high fuel prices could reduce demand and depress retail foot traffic.

High-power microwave systems manufacturer The Elmet Group (ELMT) is set to raise $100 million at a $381 million market cap. Elmet manufactures precision components and engineered systems using refractory metals such as tungsten, molybdenum, and niobium, along with high-power microwave technologies. Its products are designed for applications that require performance in extreme heat, electromagnetic intensity, and other demanding operating environments, including aerospace, defense, semiconductor equipment, medical devices, industrial systems, and energy infrastructure. The company’s operations span material processing, machining, fabrication, and engineered microwave components, giving it a vertically integrated manufacturing model that supports both custom parts and specialized system-level solutions. The Elmet Group’s non-China supply agreements limit tariff impacts and provide stability, but the company is much smaller than its peers and has just $2 million in pre-IPO cash.

data.title: Valero Energy: An Oil Refiner That Will Grow Even With An Open Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The crack spread hit its highest level since April 2022, after the start of the Ukraine-Russia war. The crack spread is the main indicator of how profitable refining operations are, representing the price difference between a barrel of oil and the refined petroleum products. The most compelling part about Valero Energy (VLO) is that it will benefit in the two scenarios that are possible in the future:

  1. The situation in the Strait of Hormuz de-escalates, and the pre-war transportation of 20% of global oil and gas consumption resumes, leading to higher volume through refiners (meaning revenue is steady or increases even if oil price drops);

  2. Or the Strait of Hormuz remains blocked and the crack elevated, with high prices compensating for lower volumes of oil that reach refineries.

In my view, there is even a third scenario we could describe, which, in my opinion, will be the one happening: the Strait of Hormuz opens, price of oil goes down, crack goes down but - and that’s the main nuance here - global demand will push to diversify away from oil supplies originating in the Gulf, similarly to how Europe diversified away from Russian gas towards US LNG (benefiting Cheniere Energy Inc. (LNG) since 2022). Now, more gas transits through the eight US LNG export terminals than what all American residents consume in gas. The US has turned into a major LNG player.

And I think Valero Energy has the potential of replicating the success story of Cheniere Energy since 2022 (see its chart above), a major winner in the diversification of LNG supply chains. Whether or not the situation in the Strait of Hormuz returns to some sort of “normal,” I believe that the US refiners may be the greatest winners of this story in the long run, and Valero Energy is right now possibly on the onset of a long-term boom thanks to these macro tailwinds.

Business Overview

Valero Energy is a US oil refiner founded in 1980 and today a leading producer of liquid transportation fuels. The business is structured in three key segments, with the largest one being Refining (Q4 2025 operating income of $1.7 billion), and then Renewable Diesel (Q4 2025 operating income of $92 million) and Ethanol ($117 million for the same time period). Clearly the refining segment is the core of the business. The $78 million drop year-over-year in operating income for Renewable Diesel was offset by the $97 million gain in Ethanol in Q4 2025 relative to Q4 2024, but both segments remain largely marginal earnings wise.

Valero Energy was one of the main beneficiaries of the Ukraine-Russia war, more than doubling in stock price within a few months. While the stock did quite some sideways since, it hit a new all-time high now after the Strait of Hormuz closure. My point is the business has shown sufficient resilience to prove that its stock isn’t just a pump and dump based solely on oil price or crack spread. This stock is suitable for investors (not traders) as, even after a sharp rise, in the long run it continues to expand its business operations and the stock to hit new all-time highs.

Solid Figures Proving That Scale Matters

As per the latest earnings report, the company largely beat estimates with EPS GAAP at $3.73 (a $0.47 beat) and revenue of $30.37 billion (an impressive $2.41 billion beat), and as you can see below, the company quite consistently beat EPS estimates, even boasting quarter-over-quarter growth while crack spread significantly dropped going into year-end to $20 (versus today’s $47).

In my opinion, the reason for these estimate beats is the belief of analysts that crack spreads are the key indicator for the earnings. But that’s not taking into account the tremendous scale and effort to gain an edge relative to competitors in attracting volume, nor does it take into account the macro tailwind I mentioned earlier in relation to the Strait of Hormuz. As you can see in the map below from Valero’s investor presentation of this month, its Gulf Coast refining capacity is significantly larger relative to its competitors, including Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC) and Phillips 66 (PSX).

While I believe these two companies will also be winners, in my view the refiner with the greatest ability to process higher volumes is more likely to be resilient to sudden drops in crack spreads. All three stocks trade at a forward PE for 2026 of around 11x, but only Valero beat estimates every quarter last year, which makes me believe that it could once again be possibly trading at a discount to analysts’ estimates. Additionally, Valero beats its two competitors on the gross profit margin (13.2% against MPC’s 10.3% and PSX’s 12.3%) and on ROC (with 7.7% against 7% and 5.4%, respectively). Valero demonstrates that scale significantly improves efficiency of operations in the oil refining sector.

As of Q4 2025, Valero boasts a healthy balance sheet with current assets of $23.2 billion, far surpassing current liabilities of $14.1 billion, thanks to a comfortable cash cushion of $4.7 billion (which, again, doesn’t take into account the likely windfall profits of Q1 and Q2 this year). This capital intensive company with total debt of $11.7 billion is rated BBB by Fitch, an investment-grade rating with a stable outlook.

These most recent hard figures all date from Q4 2025, and therefore don’t yet include the expected windfall profits from the sudden spike in crack spreads. The key growth catalyst here will be the way these windfall profits will be used. If management announces during the next quarterly call (expected on April 30 relative to Q1) that the proceeds will in large part be reinvested to expand the refinery operations, rather than simply spent in dividend payments (which is great for existing shareholders in the short-term), that will be, in my view as a long-term investor, a much more interesting confirmation of the long-term growth thesis.

Therefore, looking forward to the next earnings report relative to Q1 2026 I will be focused on the commentary on how the extra cash will be invested. Analysts on Wall Street are currently tabling on EPS GAAP of $3.25 and revenue of $30.3 billion. These figures are in line with what was reported for Q4 2025 (flat revenue and decline in EPS), which, in my view, is a strong underestimate due to the crack spreads that have soared for most of the month of March, probably significantly boosting revenue and earnings. The stock has a revisions grade on Seeking Alpha of A- with 18 up revisions in the last 90 days against only one down revision. I do think we could have a large beat again, given the crack spread went from $20 to $50 over the course of the first quarter with a median of approximately $35, while it was around $20 to $30 in the fourth quarter with a median of $25. The crack spread increased by 30%, and if we were to use this key indicator, I believe we could have an EPS higher than the fourth quarter, potentially hitting $4 and higher.

Risk Factor To My Case

As a potential shareholder and an Oil & Gas graduate, the key risk factor to this Strong Buy case I am making is if the company loses sight that this sector is cyclical and will also have tougher times ahead. If in the earnings call in two weeks management decides to spend most of the extra proceeds on rewarding its shareholders (don’t get me wrong, a portion of the windfall profits going into hiking the 2% dividend yield or into buying back shares makes absolute sense), or decides to do so later in the year, could make me reconsider the long-term growth prospects of the company. However, and we have seen it already in 2022, its shareholder-friendly management thinks long-term and continues to reinvest in the business, effectively scaling operations across the Gulf Coast and increasing the resilience of the business when crack spreads fall. I expect these reinvestments to continue, and if they do, they will mitigate my current key concern (and surely that of the market).

Bottom Line

Valero Energy is the largest oil refiner in the US and a logical potential winner from the current macro tailwinds thanks to its scale and track record, whether the Strait of Hormuz remains open or not. I rate the stock a Strong Buy and believe in the long-term growth story beyond specific events that act as short-term tailwinds. All that investors have to do now is monitor carefully what management decides to do with the extra earnings expected in Q1 and possibly Q2 of this year, at least.

With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. data.title: The Alfred E. Neuman Market Returns
data.description:
On Wednesday this week, the S&P 500 stock index closed above 7,000 for the first time ever and thus gave traders the thrill of two big things on the same day: a nice round number (oh, how we love crossing the round number thresholds), and a record close to boot!

So the stock market has clawed back all its losses since the onset of the Middle East war, and then some. Is this the dawn of another one of those magic carpet rides the market takes us on from time to time, or is there potentially cause for a pause?

Neither we nor anyone else can answer that last question with any certainty, of course. But take note of the following: Wednesday was also the day when a company called Allbirds (BIRD), which makes a line of footwear seemingly popular with the inhabitants of Silicon Valley, for reasons not entirely clear to us, announced out of nowhere that it was pivoting (a timeless expression of Valleyspeak) to become an AI compute infrastructure company.

Yep, from shoes to AI compute in one easy press release. Shares in the company rose 580 percent after the announcement (no, that is not a typo). Make of that what you will. When these types of things happen, the image that comes to our mind is that grinning visage of the 1970s, Alfred E. Neuman, on the cover of Mad Magazine with the iconic tag line “what, me worry?” Indeed.

Meanwhile, in the Actual World

Do you know what prices have not gone back to their prewar levels? Oil prices, that’s what, and they seem to have settled into a range around $95, plus or minus $5 or so, per barrel of Brent crude oil.

The question of when oil prices will come off their elevated levels probably will have a lot to do with whether the stock market continues on its merry way upwards or starts to cool off. Earlier this week the IMF came out with a fairly downbeat assessment of the war’s potential impact on global economic growth, painting several scenarios of increasing harm depending on how long energy prices remain stuck at current levels (or higher).

In the best case, which assumes that the current various ceasefires hold and activity in the Strait of Hormuz returns to something resembling normality by early summer, global growth will fall to 3.1 percent from 3.4 percent last year – a gradually slowing trend.

If energy disruptions last through the end of the year, though, the situation gets a lot more dire and the possibility of a global recession goes up, according to the agency’s analysis presented as its annual spring meetings in Washington got underway.

The stock market seems to have digested the best-case scenario with no qualms. But looking ahead to those crucial summer months, oil traders are proceeding with more caution.

According to current Brent crude futures prices posted by CME Group (the Chicago Mercantile Exchange), August 2026 futures are fetching around $88 per barrel, and that only drops to $82 per barrel for the December 2026 contract. Those prewar prices, fluctuating between $60 and $70 per barrel during the first two months of this year, seem a long way from being seen again.

Complacency versus Caution

Whether the market is being too complacent at present is something we only will learn over the next couple of months as more data come in, particularly about jobs and inflation, both of which have been trending in the wrong direction since well before the war began at the end of February.

On the caution side of the equation, we are likely to see an abundance of it from the Fed until they have a better understanding of how much the inflationary impact of the war will be structural as opposed to transitory (and don’t expect any of the governors or regional bank heads to actually say the word transitory, regardless).

The labor market is similarly perplexing, with Fed Chair Powell having noted on a number of occasions recently that, in his view, there is roughly zero net job creation growth happening now.

It might be just as perplexing to the number crunchers at the Bureau of Labor Statistics itself, given the massive revisions that seem to be coming out every month in regard to the previously published estimates of nonfarm payroll growth.

On the other hand, there has been quite a bit of upbeat commentary from bank executives this week as the major US financial institutions report Q1 financial results and offer their views on the rest of the year.

Consumer spending is ticking along and, despite high levels of consumer debt, defaults and delinquencies are more or less in line with expectations. As long as consumers keep spending and AI companies keep throwing money at their infrastructure build-out, overall growth should remain positive.

Which brings us back to that earlier sidebar about Allbirds. Watch this space, because a shoe company becoming an AI infrastructure player seems to be on par with that old parlor trick of 26-odd years ago – slapping a “dot-com” at the end of your company name and watching the share price rocket into the stratosphere. We’ve seen this rodeo before.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Can AI Chip Design Help Baidu And Alibaba Unlock Shareholder Value?
data.description:
By Henry Greene

In the age of AI, hardware is crucial. Large language models (LLMs) and other AI systems demand extreme amounts of computing power. Seemingly, the only limitation to their development is the quantity and quality of the chips that they can access.

The valuations of the world’s largest chip designers and manufacturers are a testament to the importance of chips to AI development. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing ('TSMC')(TSM) is a key manufacturer, or fabricator, of the high-end microchips that AI systems run on and has seen its share price increase 429% since ChatGPT launched in November. Meanwhile, Nvidia (NVDA), a key chip designer, also known as a fabless chipmaker, has seen its share price rise 1,270% since ChatGPT was launched.

Facing an uncertain supply of high-end chips due to US export restrictions under the Biden administration, Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) invested heavily in their own chip design capabilities. Today, these ventures have the potential to help them unlock significant shareholder value. A simple Sum of the Parts (SOTP) analysis of Baidu and Alibaba, including their respective chip design arms, Kunlunxin and T-Head, suggests markets may not be valuing these internet giants properly. Although chip design accounts for a small portion of revenue for both companies, if these businesses could command the same valuation multiples as global peers like Nvidia or China-based fabless peers like Cambricon, both businesses would be worth significantly more.

Sum of the Parts (SOTP) Analysis of Baidu & Alibaba

Baidu and Alibaba represent full-stack AI ecosystems, generating value across models, cloud, and now semiconductors. These capabilities were added on top of existing businesses in advertising and e-commerce for Baidu and Alibaba, respectively, making these companies conglomerates. Baidu’s new technological edge is also added on top of a relatively mature robotaxi business: Apollo Go.

Starting with Baidu, we conducted a sum-of-the-parts analysis to find the implied market valuation of its businesses, derived from Baidu's overall market capitalization and assumptions about these businesses' contribution to overall revenue. Baidu's chip design unit, Kunlunxin, and its robotaxi unit, Apollo Go, appear undervalued compared to the valuations the markets are currently applying to comparable businesses Cambricon and Waymo for chip design and robotaxis,2 respectively.

Alibaba appears to be in a similar situation with at least one of its business units. Its chip design unit, T-Head, if valued at a fraction of Alibaba's market cap proportionate to its revenue contribution, is also worth considerably less than Cambricon.

Exploring The Differences Between T-Head & Kunlunxin

T-Head and Kunlunxin are both fabless semiconductor companies, meaning they do not manufacture their own chips. Rather, they are customers of fabricators such as TSMC, focusing instead on chip design. Kunlunxin is estimated to have generated $500 million in revenue in 2025.3 Its revenue mix is currently split evenly between external customers and chips for internal use within Baidu’s massive cloud server network. China Mobile, one of China’s largest mobile phone carriers, is a key customer. The mobile provider was also a key strategic investor in the unit, valuing it at $3 billion when it invested in a funding round in mid-2025.3 Kunlunxin's product focus is purely on AI accelerators, specialized chips meant to speed up inference operations.

T-Head is estimated to have generated $1.5 billion in revenue in 2025. Like Kinlun, T-Head benefits from both external and internal demand. However, T-Head receives approximately 60% of its revenue from external customers, whereas Kunlunxin only receives 50% of its revenue from its external customers.

Although both Kunlunxin and T-Head have developed niches and established strong domestic, internal, and external consumption pipelines for their products, the status of US export controls may affect their performance. China approved a first batch of H200 Nvidia chips for ByteDance (BDNCE), Alibaba, and Tencent (TCEHY) in mid-January this year, after the Trump Administration departed from the previous US Administration’s stance of completely restricting the export of higher-end AI chips. However, the extent to which Nvidia chips will be available in China remains uncertain, and Kunlunxin and T-Head could step in to make up for lost demand when necessary. The continued uncertainty of overseas chip availability to China-based customers remains an opportunity for these players, alongside Cambricon.

Potential Spin-Off Scenarios

Baidu has already filed for a Hong Kong IPO of Kunlunxin. However, Alibaba has not indicated any intended change for T-Head within its corporate structure.

Below are three possible spin-off scenarios for Kunlunxin and T-Head.

Scenario #1: The parent companies could choose to keep the IPO float low and maintain a large stake, potentially a controlling one, in the business. If the spinoffs are in high demand with investors, this is an ideal strategy, as the companies would benefit most from the potential appreciation of the floated shares. This would be similar to how JD.com (JD) maintained a 50% ownership stake in its own logistics business after spinning it off as JD Logistics (JDLGF) through a Hong Kong IPO in 2021.4

Scenario #2: The parent companies could float the entirety of their fabless chipmaking units on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange and not keep a meaningful stake. We believe this is unlikely, as the units are relatively small and will find it difficult to attract sufficient outside investor demand to lead to the valuations mentioned above on their own. This would lead to a potential one-time windfall for the companies’ shareholders, which could be distributed to investors as a dividend or be reinvested into their businesses. Given Baidu’s currently high cash position, we believe the company would likely decide to distribute the windfall from the IPO as a dividend.

Scenario #3: Alibaba could decide not to follow in Baidu’s footsteps and keep its T-head unit wholly owned inside the company. This would limit the increase in its share price, though market sentiment around chip IPOs stemming from Baidu’s spinoff may potentially raise Alibaba’s valuation if its revenue share increases. The company could still decide to pursue an internal restructuring, thereby calling out T-Head as a key component of its business and breaking out its revenue consistently. Alibaba pursued a similar strategy in 2023 by breaking itself into six businesses without any spin-offs. This may have triggered investors to look at the company through the lens of an SOTP analysis. Alibaba’s stock has risen over 80% since that restructuring.²

Conclusion

Alibaba and Baidu are full-stack AI value generators if you include their chipmaking units in a holistic analysis of their businesses. A sum-of-the-parts analysis suggests that these units are undervalued relative to comparable businesses. Spinning off their chipmaking units, or at least forcing the market to value them separately, could generate significant shareholder value.

Citations:

  1. Butts, Dylan. "Baidu's semiconductor unit Kunlunxin files for Hong Kong listing amid AI chip boom in China," CNBC. January 2, 2026.
  2. Reuters. "Waymo Seeking About $16 Billion near $110 Billion Valuation, Bloomberg News Reports," Reuters. January 31, 2026.
  3. Company report as of 12/31/2025.
  4. "PROPOSED SPIN-OFF AND SEPARATE LISTING OF JD LOGISTICS INC. ON THE MAIN BOARD OF THE STOCK EXCHANGE OF HONG KONG LIMITED," JD.com Inc., HKEX News. February 16, 2021.

Original Post

data.title: Vodafone: Rotation 2 Months Ago, 'HOLD' In 2026
data.description:
I was one of the few analysts on SA who, when it was cheap, was positive on Vodafone (VOD) as an investment. At the time, about a year ago, it represented my by far largest telco holding. However, following a truly stellar climb to heights not seen for some time, I went "HOLD" on my rating and chose to rotate my entire stake in the company a few months back - a few weeks before starting my IG, Wolf of Value.

This should not be viewed as something I have against Vodafone, but rather something having to do with the company valuation, which at the time, unfortunately, had expanded to very high levels. I bought the company at less than £0.7/share for the native ticker trading on the London Stock Exchange, and my cost basis was even lower than that, even post the dividend cut, generating a very attractive dividend yield.

As it stands now, following my recent article found here, the company has performed in accordance with and alongside my expectations. I rated it at a "HOLD" at an ADR share price of about $15.1/share - the company has underperformed the SPY since that time, as you'll see in the infographic below.

Vodafone has been doing a lot of things right, as I also covered in that same article. Everything from successful mergers to growth markets offsetting legacy has been justifying this company's valuation increases. But at my latest article, the justification for higher P/E premiums stopped. As I am writing this article, Vodafone doesn't offer even a 3.4% native yield, and is "only" BBB rated, with a P/E of close to 15x. There is a decent forecast for growth for this company going forward, but me, I consider that growth to be very unlikely at this time, and consider cost pressures, inflation, and unfavorable comps to deliver a more moderate increase.

Why that is, I will tell you here.

Vodafone - Significant upside only if growth materializes, which I view as very unlikely

Let's get down to brass tacks straightaway. Vodafone is currently estimating a multi-year growth upside for the period 2028-2029E. The company is betting on a number of things to deliver this AEPS growth. First off, the Three UK merger. The expectation for the company's deal to compete with BT and O2 is expected to bring in excess of half a billion pounds of cost synergies every single year - and this estimated materialization starts in 2028, so in about 2 years. However, I consider these supposed merger synergies to be unrealistic at their current estimate. This is because of existing regulatory constraints and a chronic overestimation of cost savings. This is not just something Vodafone does - many companies do it. To give you an example, VOD means to merge about 10,000 redundant cell sites, including IT systems such as billing and the like. The complexity of such wind-downs and merging of systems often results in cost additions as well as savings, diluting the eventual goal of what it's trying to do.

Furthermore, Vodafone claimed £11B of investments for 10 years to come, once the merger is approved. The details of how these CapEx spends are going to flow are unclear - and I would refer you back to the 4-to-3 merger, such as India and Idea, for failures in exactly this. This specific move not only resulted in massive net customer add losses, but also failed to produce any sort of financial stability in the company.

Secondly, continental Europe. Vodafone has been selling off what it considers to be non-core continental assets and operations, many of them in geographies that I would personally consider to be somewhat troubled. This includes, above all, Italy and Spain. Instead, VOD is favoring higher-growth and higher-market-share geographies. When it comes to this particular part of the strategy, I cannot really argue with it - though there are some risks with doing it. As a result of the sale, Vodafone's footprint shrinks, and the footprint is a reason that Vodafone commands a fair premium over other telcos. It reflects an ignorance of scale, of revenue growth potential, which is somewhat baffling since the company actually has a strong focus on other growth markets. So, something I look at a bit dubiously at.

Selling off non-core, however, does not solve some of Vodafone's issues - including low sales growth in relation to CapEx. This weighs on all telcos, but Vodafone has seen some extreme amounts of it.

What I am presenting to you here is a case against why Vodafone will grow as expected, because the current AEPS growth estimates are extremely "lofty", to say the least. 2028E is expected to bring about 25%, and 2029E is expected to bring 50% of AEPS, with a total AEPS of upwards of £0.15/share. If that were to materialize, the company could even go back to its old dividends.

There are reasons aside from what I say above that this is unrealistic, however.

First off, the company is not apt at hitting targets historically. It quite often misses them.

Furthermore, and more importantly, none of the company's remaining European markets, excluding the UK, is likely to drive the sort of growth that would enable these sorts of returns. Vodafone expects Germany to return to significant growth. Germany is not a growth market for telcos - Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) is proof of this. It may grow at low single digits, but low single digits isn't going to be contributing the necessary upside to hit those targets. The company's focus on improving ARPU rather than just chasing net adds is laudable, but hardly unique or new. I don't see any significant reason as to why Vodafone should succeed more than its competition when it comes to this.

While African growth and AI-driven cost efficiencies are likely to be a better driver of forward upside than the above-mentioned ones, they also won't move the needle as much as seems to be expected here. Automation and AI come with system and upkeep costs, and OpenRAN comes with its own challenges. Where I actually see the clearest and most obvious upside for Vodafone is its increased capital structure improvements. Lower debt and interest will make a clear case for lower cost, which increases earnings. But again, not as much as this.

The totality of all of these upsides will, as I see it, not come close to the upside that investors seem to be seeing or citing here. It will become (quite likely) a leaner operator, improving every market. But I argue its AEPS improvements will be in the high single-digit to low double-digit range, rather than low to mid-range.

This means that any PT above a level that assumes this would be too high, especially with what is at over £1.1/share, a very conservative yield indeed for a telecommunications company, one that is in fact under the risk-free rate.

Vodafone valuation - without the AEPS growth, the annualized RoR is sub-10%

I forecast Vodafone using the 20-year average of about 12.5x, which is also in line with other telcos on the market. I give the company, for the sake of argument and positivity, a 9% annualized AEPS growth every single year until 2029.

Using that 12.5x P/E, and expecting an AEPS just below, or at £0.1/share, comes to no more than an implied price target of about £1.15/share for 2029, as the current blended P/E is closer to 16x. That means that assuming a 12.5x P/E average, and assuming even close to 10% growth per year, the annualized rate of return would be less than 7% - and that is with the 3% dividend. Without the dividend, the RoR would be closer to 2.8% per year.

That is insufficient for me, and I believe that it should be insufficient for you as well, on the basis of risk-free rates giving over 4% per year at this time.

My stance continues to be that the company is priced too high. I believe its current share price is a product of its substantial price hikes, in some cases about 4% above CPI in the UK, meaning Vodafone is pushing above-inflation price hikes. This is not necessarily a positive, because these hikes imply that the company may be able to repeat this feat. It is, however, quite unlikely. We've been looking at a 70% recovery in Vodafone in a single year. This speaks to me making the right decision from a valuation perspective, but that, in my view, also includes the right decision to "get out" of the investment at a higher price.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of about £0.95/share. I consider this, in fact, to even be a bit high here, but I'm sticking to my target, because I do believe that the company is able to do some major improvements to its balance sheet. Note that this does mean that the company is now more than 15% overvalued, and that I would continue to view it as a "HOLD" here.

The recent "flat trading" in the stock confirms to me that I made the right decision here, and I'm waiting to "get back in" at this time, wanting an upside of at least 15% per year.

In my IG, Wolf of Value, I cover other telcos similar to VOD that I consider to be more attractive than this one, as well as other interesting investments for both conservative, income-oriented investors, as well as more growth-oriented investors.

Risks & Considerations

The biggest risk with Vodafone is now in the medium term, now that short-term risks have been somewhat overcome. The combination of continued macro pressure compounded with volatility in its markets tells me that the company has come as far as it can go.

Aside from fundamental risks to its geographies, I view Vodafone's current risk profile as being impacted by overreliance on the German segment for stable revenues. Despite investments, multiple telcos have shown us that finding growth or even stability in Germany is very hard. I do not view the growth markets as the company's main problems, if things happen there, but the impacts of a lower or anemic return in its legacy. I don't think it would necessarily hurt Vodafone over the short term, but I do believe the market reaction would be to lower the share price, and I maintain this view as of April 2026.

In this case, and as of this article update, Vodafone is a "HOLD". My position, as it stands, is rotated.

Thesis

Vodafone is a qualitative, Europe-covering telco with an African and geographical growth vector. The company has a decent yield, though much lower now than before. It no longer has the sort of upside that I would be interested in investing in.

The company is no longer cheap or attractive here at more than £1/share and at close to 15x P/E. The risk/reward consideration needs to be looked at in conjunction with things like the 2026E forecast, which is negative. I no longer see market-beating upside, and for that reason, the company becomes a "HOLD", with my recent article marking the point where I sold my stake.

My updated PT for the company is £0.95, meaning a "HOLD", This comes to about $11.45/share for the ADR VOD.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of $11.45/share, I don't see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I rate the company a "HOLD" rating as of February 2026 and as of Q3 2026.

data.title: An Important Lesson For Investors
data.description:
Originally published on April 15, 2026

Just over two weeks ago, on March 30th, every single key US index ETF that we track in our Trend Analyzer tool was oversold, with all but the Dow 30 ETF in extreme oversold territory.

Fear was permeating, and bearish sentiment gauges were rising sharply.

Fast forward to two weeks later. As shown below, these same index ETFs are now all in overbought territory (with the exception of the Dow 30).

For investors and traders, the action over the last few weeks is a good reminder that markets don’t stay oversold or overbought forever.

If you’re fearful when prices get oversold or ebullient when prices get overbought, try to remind yourself that the script will eventually flip.

Original Post
